name: Hou Yi
description: Hou Yi, also known as Shen Yi, is a prominent figure in Chinese mythology, celebrated as a divine archer and hero. He is most famously known for shooting down nine of the ten suns that threatened to scorch the earth, an act of profound heroism that saved humanity from annihilation. While sometimes portrayed as a god, Hou Yi is also depicted as a mortal who acquired divine skills and weapons, blurring the lines between the divine and the human. His legend embodies themes of courage, selflessness, and the importance of maintaining cosmic order. Subsequent narratives delve into his relationship with Chang'e, the moon goddess, and the tragic consequences of his pursuit of immortality, adding layers of complexity to his character. He remains a significant cultural icon, representing skill, strength, and the potential for humans to overcome even the most overwhelming challenges. His story serves as a cautionary tale about ambition and the balance between human and divine realms.
subtitle: The Divine Archer

keyMyths.title: Shooting Down the Suns
keyMyths.description: The most famous myth surrounding Hou Yi recounts how ten suns, sons of the Jade Emperor, rose into the sky simultaneously, scorching the earth and causing immense suffering. The Jade Emperor summoned Hou Yi, granting him a magical bow and arrows. With incredible skill, Hou Yi shot down nine of the suns, leaving only one to provide light and warmth to the world. This act established Hou Yi as a great hero and savior of humanity, restoring balance to the cosmos.
keyMyths.source: Werner, E. T. C. *Myths and Legends of China*. George G. Harrap & Co., 1922.

keyMyths.title: Hou Yi and Chang'e
keyMyths.description: After saving the world, Hou Yi was rewarded with an elixir of immortality. However, his wife, Chang'e, consumed the elixir, either accidentally or intentionally (accounts vary). As a result, she floated to the moon, where she became the Moon Goddess. Hou Yi was heartbroken and visited the moon every night, leaving offerings for his lost love. This myth is the origin of the Mid-Autumn Festival and the mooncake tradition.

keyMyths.title: Hou Yi's Demise
keyMyths.description: Some versions of the legend detail Hou Yi's downfall. After achieving fame and power, he became tyrannical and oppressive. In one account, he was murdered by his apprentice, Feng Meng, who sought to usurp his archery skills and position. This tragic end serves as a cautionary tale about the corrupting influence of power and the importance of humility, even for the greatest of heroes. It also highlights the cyclical nature of heroism and the potential for even saviors to fall from grace.
keyMyths.source: Yang, Lihui, et al. *Handbook of Chinese Mythology*. Oxford University Press, 2005.

sources.author: Werner, E. T. C.
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends, providing detailed accounts of Hou Yi's exploits and his significance in Chinese folklore.
